back_translation: Jesus entered the city of Jericho and was passing through.
Behold, there was a man named Zacchaeus; he was a chief tax collector and was a wealthy man.
He tried to see who Jesus was, but because of the crowd he could not see, since he was short in stature.
So he ran ahead and climbed a sycamore-fig tree in order to see him, for he would pass by that way.
When Jesus came to that place, he lifted up his face and said to him, 'Zacchaeus, hurry down, for today I must stay at your house.'
He hurried down and welcomed him with joy.
When everyone saw, they all grumbled, saying, 'He has gone in to lodge with a sinner.'
But Zacchaeus stood up and said to the Lord, 'Lord, look, my possessions — I give half to the poor, and if I have cheated anything from anyone, I will return it fourfold.'
Jesus said to him, 'Today salvation has come to this house, because he too is a son of Abraham.'
'For the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost.'
While they were listening to this, Jesus spoke another parable, because he was near Jerusalem and because they thought the kingdom of God would appear immediately.
He said, 'A certain nobleman traveled to a distant land to receive the authority of kingship, and then to return.'
'He called ten of his servants and gave them ten minas of money, and said, "Take this money and do business until I return."'
'But the people of his city hated him and sent a delegation after him. They said, "We do not want this man to rule over us."'
'Later, when he returned after being appointed king, he ordered those servants to whom he had given the money to be called, so that he might know how much profit each one had made from trading.'
'The first servant came and said, "Sir, your mina has made ten more minas."'
'The king said to him, "Very good, good servant, because you have been faithful in a small thing, you shall have authority over ten cities."'
'The second servant came and said, "Sir, your mina has made five more minas."'
'The king said to this one, "You shall be ruler of five cities."'
'Another came and said, "Sir, look, here is your mina, which I kept wrapped in a cloth.'
'"For I was afraid of you, because you are a strict man — you take what you did not lay down and reap what you did not sow."'
'The king said to him, "Wicked servant, I will judge you by your own words. Did you know that I am a strict man, taking what I did not lay down and reaping what I did not sow?'
'"Then why did you not deposit my money in a bank? When I returned, I would have received the money back with interest."'
'Then the king said to those standing nearby, "Take that mina from him and give it to the one who has ten minas."'
'They said to him, "Sir, he has ten minas already."'
'"I tell you all, to everyone who has, more will be given; but from the one who does not have, even what he has will be taken away from him.'
'"As for these enemies of mine who did not want me to rule over them, bring them here and execute them before me."'
When Jesus had said these things, he went on ahead going up to Jerusalem.
And when Jesus drew near to the villages of Bethphage and Bethany near the mountain called the Mount of Olives, he sent out two of his disciples.
He said, 'Go into the village that is ahead. When you enter, you will find a young donkey tied up, on which no human has ever sat. Untie the rope and lead it here.'
'If anyone asks you, "Why are you untying the colt?" you shall answer, "The Lord has need to use it."'
Those who were sent went out and found it just as he had told them.
While they were untying the young donkey, the owners of the donkey asked them, 'Why are you untying the young donkey?'
They answered, 'The Lord has need to use it.'
They led the young donkey to Jesus, then took their cloaks and spread them on the donkey, and invited Jesus to mount on it.
As he went along, the people spread their cloaks on the road.
When he drew near to the descent from the Mount of Olives, all the disciples began to praise God loudly with joy, because of all the miracles they had seen.
They said, 'May the king who comes in the name of the Lord live long. May there be peace in heaven, and may there be glory in the highest.'
Some Pharisees in the crowd said to him, 'Teacher, please restrain your disciples.'
He answered, 'I tell you all, if these are silent, the stones will cry out.'
When he drew near and beheld the city, he wept over it.
He said, 'If you, even you, had known on this day the things that bring you peace, it would have been good — but now this is hidden from your eyes.'
'For days will come upon you when your enemies will set up a siege-rampart around you, will encircle you, and press you in from every side.'
'They will destroy you and your children within you to the ground, and will not leave even one stone on another in you, because you did not recognize the time that God visited you.'
When Jesus entered the temple courtyard, he began to drive out those who were selling there.
He said to them, 'It is written, "My house shall be a house of prayer," but you have made it a den of robbers.'
He taught in the temple courtyard every day, but the chief priests, scribes, and leaders of the people sought a way to kill him.
But they could not find a way to do it, because all the people listened to him attentively.
